title: JavaScript every()
tldr: Array.every() tests whether all elements in the array pass the provided test function.
Returns true if every element passes — short-circuits and returns false on the first failure.
laymanDefinition: Imagine you're a quality inspector checking items on an assembly line. Your rule is 'All items must be undamaged.' You start checking each item. The moment you find a single damaged item, you stop and report 'No, not all items pass.' You don't need to check the rest. Array.every() works the same way — it checks each element until one fails, then immediately returns false.

deepDive.heading: How every() Works
deepDive.text: Array.every() iterates over each element and calls the callback with (element, index, array). If the callback returns a falsy value for any element, every() immediately returns false and stops iterating (short-circuit). If the callback returns truthy for every element, every() returns true.

deepDive.heading: Vacuous Truth — Empty Array
deepDive.text: every() on an empty array returns true. This is called 'vacuous truth' — the statement 'all elements satisfy this condition' is true when there are no elements to violate it. This is consistent with mathematical logic: 'All unicorns are pink' is true because no non-pink unicorn exists to disprove it.

deepDive.heading: Short-Circuiting Behavior
deepDive.text: every() short-circuits on the first false — it stops iterating as soon as it finds a failing element. This is the inverse of some(), which short-circuits on the first true. If you have a large array and most elements will pass, every() is efficient because it stops at the first failure.

deepDive.heading: every() vs some() — De Morgan's Law
deepDive.text: !arr.every(fn) is equivalent to arr.some(function(x) { return !fn(x); }). And !arr.some(fn) is equivalent to arr.every(function(x) { return !fn(x); }). This is De Morgan's Law applied to array testing: 'Not all passed' means 'At least one failed'. Understanding this relationship helps choose the right method.

interviewAnswer: Array.every() tests whether all elements pass a test function, returning true or false. It short-circuits — returns false immediately on the first failure. On an empty array, it returns true (vacuous truth). It does not mutate the original array. Common use cases: form validation (all fields valid?), data integrity checks (all required properties present?), permission checks (all users have required role?), and comparison checks (all elements equal a value). The key distinction from some() is that every() requires ALL elements to pass (logical AND), while some() requires at least one (logical OR).

interviewQuestions.question: What does Array.every() do?
interviewQuestions.answer: every() tests whether all elements in the array pass the provided test function. Returns true if all pass, false if any fail.

interviewQuestions.question: What does every() return on an empty array?
interviewQuestions.answer: true. This is vacuous truth — no element exists that could fail the test, so the statement 'all elements pass' is true.

interviewQuestions.question: Does every() short-circuit?
interviewQuestions.answer: Yes. every() stops iterating as soon as it finds an element that fails the test. It returns false immediately without processing remaining elements.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between every() and some()?
interviewQuestions.answer: every() requires ALL elements to pass (returns true only if all pass). some() requires AT LEAST ONE to pass (returns true if any passes).

interviewQuestions.question: Does every() mutate the original array?
interviewQuestions.answer: No. every() is read-only and does not modify the original array.

interviewQuestions.question: What arguments does the every() callback receive?
interviewQuestions.answer: Three arguments: currentValue, index, and the original array.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you check if all numbers in an array are even?
interviewQuestions.answer: arr.every(n => n % 2 === 0). Returns true if all are even, false if any is odd.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the relationship between every() and some()?
interviewQuestions.answer: De Morgan's Law: !arr.every(fn) === arr.some(x => !fn(x)). And !arr.some(fn) === arr.every(x => !fn(x)).

interviewQuestions.question: Can every() be used with arrays of objects?
interviewQuestions.answer: Yes: users.every(u => u.age >= 18) checks if all users are adults. This is a common pattern for data validation.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you implement a simple every() function?
interviewQuestions.answer: function every(arr, fn) { for (var i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) { if (!fn(arr[i], i, arr)) return false; } return true; }

codeExamples.title: Basic every() Usage
codeExamples.useCase: Checking if all elements meet a condition
codeExamples.description: every() tests all elements until one fails. Empty arrays always return true (vacuous truth).

codeExamples.title: Form Validation with every()
codeExamples.description: every() is perfect for validating that ALL conditions are met before form submission.

codeExamples.title: Data Integrity and Comparison Checks
codeExamples.description: every() is ideal for data integrity checks — ensure all records have required fields and pass validation rules.

codeExamples.title: Performance and Optimization Patterns
codeExamples.useCase: Using every() for early exit optimization
codeExamples.description: every() is more performant than filter() for existence checks because it short-circuits on the first failure.

codeExamples.title: Manual every() Implementation
codeExamples.description: A manual implementation shows every() short-circuits on first false and returns true for empty arrays.

mcqQuestions.question: What does Array.every() return?
mcqQuestions.explanation: every() returns a boolean: true if all elements pass the test, false if any fail.

mcqQuestions.question: What does [1, 2, 3].every(n => n > 0) return?
mcqQuestions.explanation: All elements are greater than 0, so every() returns true.

mcqQuestions.question: What does [].every(n => false) return?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Every() on an empty array always returns true (vacuous truth) — no element exists to fail the test.

mcqQuestions.question: Does every() short-circuit?
options: Yes, it stops at the first failure
mcqQuestions.explanation: every() short-circuits — it returns false immediately upon finding the first failing element.

mcqQuestions.question: What is the difference between every() and some()?
options: every() checks if ALL pass; some() checks if ANY pass
every() checks if ANY pass; some() checks if ALL pass
every() returns an array; some() returns a boolean
mcqQuestions.explanation: every() is like AND (all must pass). some() is like OR (at least one must pass).

mcqQuestions.question: How do you check if all numbers in [2, 4, 5, 6] are even?
mcqQuestions.explanation: arr.every(n => n % 2 === 0) returns false because 5 is odd.

mcqQuestions.question: Does every() mutate the original array?
options: Only if the callback mutates it
mcqQuestions.explanation: every() is read-only. It does not modify the original array.

mcqQuestions.question: What arguments does the every() callback receive?
mcqQuestions.explanation: The callback receives currentValue, index, and the array being iterated.

mcqQuestions.question: What is a common use case for every()?
options: Checking if all form fields are valid
mcqQuestions.explanation: every() is commonly used for validation — ensure all fields pass before allowing form submission.

mcqQuestions.question: How do you check if an array contains only strings?
mcqQuestions.explanation: arr.every(item => typeof item === 'string') is the most direct way to check if all elements are strings.
